scenario_results.reason: The lane used realistic business-services artifacts, sampled work, SLA evidence, and reviewer notes to produce a bounded service-quality review note.
The lane preserved hold posture and identified missing rework and second-review evidence before release could be supported.
Release pressure did not override the unresolved nonconformance or complaint, and unresolved risk was escalated.
Recurring indexing errors were handled as repeat-defect evidence requiring CAPA escalation rather than informal closure.
The safety-sensitive incident was routed to safety and incident owners without OSHA recordability or safety signoff claims.
The lane blocked finding closure where sampled workpaper, review notes, CAPA evidence, and effectiveness checks were missing.
The lane rejected complete client business-process redesign authority and preserved the support-service quality boundary.
The lane routed the HR placement decision to staffing or HR operations while retaining only quality evidence handling.
Low-confidence handling correctly requested SOW, acceptance criteria, quality plan, sampled cases, queue owner, and system links before any conclusion.
The SOW and SOP release-rule conflict was escalated without legal or contract interpretation.
Tenant adaptation preserved the public baseline while mapping call-quality, defect-taxonomy, SLA, and retrieval assumptions.
Conflicting service-organization control evidence was summarized and routed to audit and control owners without SOC attestation claims.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the strict 0.92 threshold for business-services quality and assurance handling.
The package explicitly keeps runtime strategy at specification-based and does not claim a retained implementation.
